cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out this question. The question is about understanding a headline from the article. The headline is "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to recall what the article said. The article talks about Dinara Safina, who used to be overshadowed by her brother Marat Safin. Now she's the world No.1. The headline in question is from the Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open, and it's mentioned that it summarizes her volatile emotional state. So, the phrase "basket case" is an idiom. I think "basket case" typically refers to someone who is very nervous, anxious, or emotionally unstable. So the headline is probably saying that Safina was once very unstable or struggling (a basket case) but then rose to the top (top of the world). Looking at the options: A) She wanted to top the world when she was a baby in a basket. But "basket case" isn't about being in a basket as a baby. That seems like a literal interpretation which is probably incorrect. B) After winning the Australian Open, she cried. The headline doesn't mention crying, just the transition from being a basket case to success. C) Too nervous at first, but finally gained confidence and won. This aligns with the idea of being a basket case (nervous) and then becoming successful. The article also mentions her emotional explosiveness and volatility, so maybe she overcame that.
